title: Profiteroles
subtitle: Choux puffs filled with whipped cream and served with warm chocolate sauce
excerpt: Twenty-four profiteroles: small rounds of choux paste piped and baked until hollow, filled with whipped double cream, and served with a warm dark chocolate ganache poured over at the table. Best assembled within 30 minutes of serving.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the cream bun section documents small choux rounds baked hollow and filled with sweetened whipped cream. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton documents choux paste made on the hob by the same method as éclairs, formed into small rounds for cream buns. Serving with a poured chocolate sauce is documented in the British and French domestic cookery literature from the late Victorian period onward, as the profiterole became a standard dinner-party dessert.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled baked shells in a sealed bag. Re-crisp in a 180°C fan oven for 8 minutes before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the shells up to 6 hours ahead and store uncovered at room temperature — do not seal them or they soften. Fill within 30 minutes of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top third; reduce to 170°C fan after 15 minutes to dry the shells

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of hot cream poured over chopped chocolate, stirred from the centre outward until smooth and emulsified. The ratio of cream to chocolate determines the consistency: equal parts by weight produces a thick, pourable ganache suitable for a sauce or glaze; more cream makes it thinner; more chocolate makes it firmer and suitable for truffles. For profiteroles, equal parts produces the right viscosity to pour at the table.

content.text: Profiteroles use the same choux paste as éclairs, shaped into rounds instead of fingers. The shells are lighter work than éclairs because there is no crème pâtissière: whipped double cream goes in cold, and the chocolate sauce (a simple
) is made in minutes and poured at the table while still warm. The timing constraint is real: filled profiteroles should be eaten within 30 minutes, before the cream softens the shells.

attrs.title: Pipe consistent rounds
attrs.body: Consistent sizing means consistent baking. Pipe rounds about 3 cm in diameter, then use a dampened finger to smooth down any peaks — peaks burn before the rest of the shell is cooked. If your rounds are irregular, sort them onto two trays by size and bake each tray separately.

items.prepNote: large, at room temperature, beaten together

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Combine the {{water}}, {{whole-milk}} milk, {{unsalted-butter}} butter, and {{sea-salt-fine}} salt in a medium saucepan over medium-high heat. Bring to a full boil. Remove from the heat and tip in all the {{plain-flour}} sifted flour at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the paste forms a smooth ball and pulls away from the pan. Return to low heat for 1–2 minutes to dry slightly. Cool for 5 minutes.

Add the {{eggs}} beaten egg a tablespoon at a time, beating fully after each addition, until the paste is glossy and falls from a lifted spoon in a thick V-shape. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a plain 1 cm nozzle. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Pipe 24 rounds about 3 cm in diameter, leaving 4 cm between each. Smooth any peaks with a damp fingertip.

Bake at 200°C fan for 15 minutes — do not open the door. Reduce to 170°C fan and bake for a further 13–15 minutes until deep golden and firm when pressed. Pierce each puff with a skewer at the side to release steam. Transfer to a rack and cool completely before filling.
Whip the cream and make the ganache

Whip the {{double-cream}} cream to soft peaks, adding {{caster-sugar}} sugar if using. Refrigerate until needed. For the ganache, bring the remaining {{double-cream}} cream to a simmer, pour over the {{dark-chocolate}} chopped chocolate, leave for 2 minutes, then stir outward from the centre until smooth. Keep warm over a bowl of hot water.

Cut each profiterole two-thirds of the way through with scissors or a serrated knife, leaving a hinge. Pipe or spoon whipped cream into the opening. Arrange on a serving plate and pour the warm ganache over at the table. Serve immediately.
